steps.specialNote: عِلْمٌ (indefinite, marfu) → الْعِلْمُ (definite, marfu) → بِعِلْمِهِ (majroor with pronoun). Watch the ending shift — it is the same word in a different grammatical role.

ayahCards.arabicText: قَالُوا سُبْحَانَكَ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَا
ayahCards.translation: They said, 'Exalted are You; we have no knowledge except what You have taught us.'

ayahCards.arabicText: وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِمَا شَاءَ
ayahCards.translation: And they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills.

steps.specialNote: سَلَامٌ (marfu, indefinite) is used as a greeting and in Quranic declarations of peace. The root س-ل-م also gives us مُسْلِم (one who submits) and إِسْلَام.

ayahCards.translation: Peace it is until the emergence of dawn.
And peace is upon me the day I was born.

steps.question: لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا — The word عِلْمَ ends in ـَ (fatha). Why?
choices: Mansoob (accusative) — لَا makes the following word accusative
steps.explanation: لَا (of negation/lā an-nafiya lil-jins) puts the following Ism into the Mansoob case — shown by the fatha ending ـَ. This is a specific grammatical construction.

steps.question: بِشَيْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِ — عِلْمِهِ ends in ـِ. What causes this?
choices: Majroor — the preposition مِنْ governs it
Mansoob — it is the object of a verb
The ـهِ suffix changes the case
steps.explanation: مِنْ is a preposition, and prepositions always assign the Majroor case (kasra ـِ) to the noun that follows. The ـهِ is a possessive suffix meaning 'his' — it does not change the case.

steps.verseTranslation: ___ it is until the emergence of dawn.
